skill.name: agile-product-owner
skill.description: Agile product ownership toolkit for Product Owners, including INVEST user story generation, sprint planning, backlog prioritization, and velocity-oriented planning support.
skill.summary: Generate user stories, acceptance criteria, sprint plans, and backlog priorities for agile product work.
skill.author: alirezarezvani

security_audit.summary: The high weak-cryptography findings are false positives caused by words such as description, processing, redesign, and architect in product-management text. The Ruby backtick findings are Markdown inline command examples for a local Python helper, not Ruby execution; no network access, filesystem writes, environment reads, subprocess use, dynamic execution, or prompt injection text was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Matches in Generated Evaluation Data
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm matches in eval_result.json, including duplicate hits on lines 24 and 66. These lines contain marketplace text and prior audit explanation text, not cryptographic API use, credential handling, or secret processing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible natural-language descriptions, examples, and audit text. No cryptographic library, hash function, encryption primitive, or security-sensitive data flow appears at these locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Matches in User Story Script
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm matches in scripts/user_story_generator.py, including a duplicate hit on line 57. The cited code performs dictionary reads, title generation, text parsing, keyword checks, and sample epic construction for agile planning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script imports only json, typing, and sys, and the cited lines are plain string or dictionary operations. I found no weak algorithm use such as MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or custom crypto.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: SKILL.md documents how to run the local Python helper inside Markdown inline code spans. These examples are not Ruby backtick execution, but they do show that the skill expects a user or agent to run a local script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text appears in Markdown usage documentation and is not executable source code. The commands are direct invocations of the included Python script with no shell interpolation or untrusted command construction.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Skill Description Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md frontmatter description contains product-owner capability text. It does not contain cryptographic logic, hidden instructions, executable code, or sensitive data handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a YAML description field and is clearly non-executable metadata. The static match is caused by words inside normal marketplace description text.

low_findings.title: Low Risk: Local Python Helper Script
low_findings.description: The skill includes a Python helper script with a command-line entry point. The reviewed code generates sample agile stories and sprint plans, prints output, and does not perform network requests, file writes, subprocess execution, environment access, or dynamic code execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The local script is real executable code, so it remains a low operational risk. Confidence is high that it is benign because the entry point only parses a numeric capacity argument and prints generated planning output.

content.user_title: Generate Agile Stories and Plan Sprints
content.value_statement: Product teams need clear stories, acceptance criteria, and realistic sprint plans. This skill turns epics into structured backlog items and capacity-based sprint plans.
actual_capabilities: Generates user stories from predefined epic data and personas.
Creates acceptance criteria in Given-When-Then and requirement formats.
Checks user stories against INVEST criteria.
Estimates story points from feature complexity keywords.
Prioritizes stories using persona and feature indicators.
Builds sample sprint plans from capacity and backlog size.
limitations: Uses predefined sample epic data unless the script is modified.
Does not integrate directly with Jira, Linear, Azure DevOps, or similar tools.
Does not store sprint history, burndown data, or team velocity over time.
Story estimates are heuristic and still require team review.

use_cases.title: Prepare Backlog Refinement
use_cases.description: Break an epic into draft stories with acceptance criteria, estimates, priorities, and INVEST checks.

use_cases.title: Plan Sprint Capacity
use_cases.description: Create a sprint plan that separates committed stories from stretch goals based on point capacity.

use_cases.title: Review Story Quality
use_cases.description: Use generated INVEST checks and estimates to identify stories that need refinement before planning.

prompt_templates.title: Draft User Stories
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate user stories for this epic: {epic_name}. Include personas, acceptance criteria, priority, and story points.

prompt_templates.title: Refine Acceptance Criteria
prompt_templates.prompt: Improve the acceptance criteria for this story: {story}. Use clear Given-When-Then criteria and include error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Sprint Scope
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a sprint with {capacity} points using this backlog: {backlog}. Separate committed work from stretch goals.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Backlog Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this backlog for INVEST quality, dependency risk, estimate size, and sprint readiness: {backlog}. Recommend specific changes.

output_examples.input: Generate stories for a dashboard epic.
output: Story: View key metrics and KPIs.
Acceptance criteria include access, successful completion, validation, error handling, performance, and keyboard navigation.
INVEST check marks the story as independent, negotiable, valuable, estimable, small, and testable.

output_examples.input: Plan a sprint with 30 points of capacity.
output: Committed work: stories that fit within capacity after priority and size sorting.
Stretch goals: stories that fit within a 20 percent capacity buffer.
The result includes total committed points and capacity utilization.

output_examples.input: Review a complex feature for story readiness.
output: The feature receives a larger estimate when it includes words such as advanced, integrate, migrate, redesign, refactor, or architect.
The INVEST check flags dependency language and vague testability language for review.
The team should split oversized stories before sprint commitment.

best_practices: Treat generated stories as drafts and validate them with the product team.
Review point estimates with developers before sprint commitment.
Update personas, scope items, and technical requirements before using the script for a real backlog.
anti_patterns: Using generated estimates as final commitments without team discussion.
Copying sample epic output directly into production planning tools.
Skipping INVEST review for stories with dependencies or vague outcomes.

faq.question: What does this skill generate?
faq.answer: It generates draft user stories, acceptance criteria, INVEST checks, priority labels, story point estimates, and sample sprint plans.

faq.question: Does it connect to Jira or Linear?
faq.answer: No. It produces local text output and does not call external project management services.

faq.question: How are story points estimated?
faq.answer: The script uses feature keywords to choose simple, medium, large, or very large estimates.

faq.question: Can I use custom personas?
faq.answer: Yes, but the predefined personas must be edited in the script before generation.

faq.question: Is it safe to run?
faq.answer: The reviewed script prints generated planning output and does not access networks, files, environment variables, or subprocesses.

faq.question: Should teams rely on the output without review?
faq.answer: No. Product owners and delivery teams should review scope, estimates, and acceptance criteria before planning.
